---
title: Deep-Learning-From-Scratch-Notes
author: Brench
date: 2026-05-08
date-modified: today
comments: false

listing:
  contents:
    - chapter01-introduction/*.qmd
    - chatper02-pytorch-introduction/*.qmd
  sort: title asc
  type: table
  fields: [title, author, date]
  date-format: iso

open-graph:
  title: Deep-Learning-From-Scratch-Notes
  description: "Deep-Learning-From-Scratch-Notes: Table of Contents"
---

<script id="academic-home-data" type="application/json">
{
  "kicker": "Modern Academic Notes",
  "title": "Deep-Learning-From-Scratch-Notes",
  "lead": "These notes connect mathematical derivations, minimal implementations, and PyTorch engineering practice in one reading path: clarify the formula, trace the diagram, then run the code.",
  "labels": {
    "all": "All",
    "parts": "Parts",
    "chapters": "Chapters",
    "topics": "Topics",
    "mapTitle": "Learning Map",
    "filter": "Filter chapters",
    "read": "Open chapter",
    "derivationTitle": "Derivation Lens"
  },
  "parts": [
    {
      "id": "foundation",
      "shortTitle": "Foundations",
      "chapters": [
        {
          "title": "1.1 Neural Networks: A Learnable Function",
          "summary": "View neural networks as function approximators, connecting parameters, layers, and nonlinear mappings.",
          "href": "chapter01-introduction/chapter-1.1-neural-network.html",
          "tags": ["function", "parameters", "nonlinearity"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.1 Automatic Differentiation in PyTorch",
          "summary": "Understand backpropagation through computation graphs, tensors, gradients, and leaf nodes.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.1-automatic-differentiation.html",
          "tags": ["autograd", "gradient", "graph"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.2 PyTorch Dimension Transformation Operations",
          "summary": "Read reshape, view, permute, and related operations through tensor layout and shape semantics.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.2-dim-transform-appendix.html",
          "tags": ["tensor", "shape", "layout"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.3 Gradient Recording and Control in PyTorch",
          "summary": "Compare training, inference, and frozen-parameter contexts through their gradient behavior.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.3-auto-grad-contexts.html",
          "tags": ["no_grad", "inference", "context"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.4 Dataset, DataLoader, and Batching",
          "summary": "Move from dataset protocols to batching pipelines for reproducible data loading.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.4-dataset-and-dataloader.html",
          "tags": ["data", "batch", "pipeline"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.5 nn.Module: Models, Parameters, and State",
          "summary": "Understand module trees, parameter registration, buffers, and forward calls.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.5-nn-module.html",
          "tags": ["module", "state", "parameter"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.6 Optimizer: From Manual Updates to State",
          "summary": "Use optimizer state to reason about learning rates, momentum, and parameter groups.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.6-optimizer.html",
          "tags": ["optimizer", "state", "lr"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.7 Training Loop",
          "summary": "Connect forward passes, loss, backward passes, updates, and evaluation in one stable loop.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.7-training-loop.html",
          "tags": ["training", "loss", "loop"]
        },
        {
          "title": "2.8 Checkpoints and Resuming",
          "summary": "Save models, optimizers, random states, and progress so experiments can resume and reproduce.",
          "href": "chatper02-pytorch-introduction/chapter-2.8-checkpoints-resuming.html",
          "tags": ["checkpoint", "resume", "reproducibility"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "modes": [
    {
      "id": "formula",
      "mark": "F",
      "label": "Formula",
      "title": "Start with the relation between objects",
      "body": "Each topic first pins down notation, tensor shapes, and the objective so the formula becomes the interface for the implementation."
    },
    {
      "id": "diagram",
      "mark": "D",
      "label": "Diagram",
      "title": "Trace how data moves",
      "body": "Layers, tensors, gradients, and state are treated as a flow that can be inspected step by step."
    },
    {
      "id": "code",
      "mark": "C",
      "label": "Code",
      "title": "End with a minimal runnable implementation",
      "body": "Implementations stay short and readable: verify behavior first, then discuss engineering abstractions.",
      "code": "loss.backward()\noptimizer.step()\noptimizer.zero_grad()"
    }
  ]
}
</script>

<div id="academic-home-app" class="academic-home"></div>

<noscript>
<div class="academic-noscript">
  <p>The learning map uses JavaScript; the static table of contents remains available below.</p>
</div>
</noscript>

<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/vue@3/dist/vue.global.prod.js"></script>
<script src="../assets/js/academic-home.js"></script>
